moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual first move—the Van Geet Opening, where we develop our knight before controlling the center with pawns. This move is flexible and somewhat offbeat, aiming to support central play while keeping Black guessing about our setup. The knight on c3 is well-placed to support e4 or d5 later, and it avoids the main-line theory that Black has probably prepared. While unconventional, this move has been used by strong players to sidestep prepared openings and create dynamic positions where tactical play matters more than deep theoretical knowledge.

Black immediately strikes with the Laroche Gambit, a sharp counterattacking try that challenges our central intentions right away. By pushing the b-pawn forward, Black threatens to grab space on the queenside and opens lines for the bishop on c8—notice how this move prepares ...Bb7, where the bishop will eye the e4 square and the long diagonal. This is a principled and aggressive response that says Black isn't content to let us dictate the game's character; instead of playing defensively, Black creates immediate counterplay and forces us to decide whether to accept the pawn tension or find a quieter setup.
